debit_card_txn.narrative: Debit card POS transactions show a critical divergence: purchase volumes are stabilising with YoY decline moderating to -14.4% (the smallest in 17 months), suggesting the channel may be approaching a sustainable floor rather than terminal collapse. Acquirers should reassess terminal economics for high-ticket merchant segments where UPI limits bind. In sharp contrast, the cash-at-POS feature is operationally extinct — May 2026 volume of 7,941 transactions is the all-time low, down 99% from Jan 2024. Despite a statistical YoY growth artifact (+17.3% value, -38.0% volume), absolute levels confirm complete user abandonment. Issuers must sunset immediately: the feature consumes operational resources for zero strategic value. Redirect users to ATMs and eliminate infrastructure costs.

The moderation is a base effect: May 2025 was 12,800 transactions, near the collapse floor; May 2026 at 7,941 is 38% below that trough but still 99% below Jan 2024 levels.
The YoY improvement reflects a low base, not a recovery — absolute volume remains at the all-time minimum.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-yoy.inference: Ignore the YoY moderation — it is a statistical artifact, not a strategic signal. The channel remains operationally extinct with absolute volume at record lows. Maintain sunset plans: the positive trajectory will reverse as the base normalises, and no demand recovery is underway.

infrastructure.narrative: India's digital acceptance infrastructure is bifurcating: UPI QR codes are in sustained expansion at 779.8M units (+16.4% YoY), adding 9M codes monthly, while POS terminals have entered year-on-year contraction at -0.5% — the first negative reading on record. The UPI-to-POS ratio hit 67.7×, a record divergence, as UPI QR scaled 115× faster than POS in absolute terms. Bharat QR is obsolete at 139× fewer codes than UPI QR. Lenders financing merchant acceptance should allocate exclusively to UPI QR infrastructure — POS is a shrinking asset class with no recovery signal, and the gap is widening every month.

atm-offsite-abs.title: Offsite ATMs at 73,964 — second monthly gain, still near record low
atm-offsite-abs.observation: Offsite ATM count stands at 73,964 in May 2026, up 107 units from Apr's 73,857.
atm-offsite-abs.direction: Rose 73,857 → 73,964 in May — the second consecutive monthly gain after a 15-period contraction streak ended in Apr.
chain: Offsite ATMs added 107 units in May, following Apr's 164-unit gain — the first back-to-back increases since late 2024.
The network remains near its all-time low, down 20% from Jan 2024's 93,381, as banks have permanently closed unprofitable locations.
Two months of growth do not reverse the structural decline — digital channels continue absorbing transaction volumes, making offsite ATMs economically unviable.
atm-offsite-abs.inference: The May gain extends Apr's pause but does not signal a reversal — offsite ATMs remain at their lowest level on record, and banks have no incentive to rebuild this network. The two-month stabilisation likely reflects the completion of the closure wave, not a strategic shift. Expect the network to plateau near 74K as banks redirect capital to onsite terminals.

atm-offsite-yoy.title: Offsite ATMs -8.87% YoY — contraction moderating for fifth straight month
atm-offsite-yoy.observation: Offsite ATMs contracted 8.87% year-on-year in May 2026, improving 1.64 points from Apr's -10.50%.
atm-offsite-yoy.direction: YoY decline improved -14.0% → -12.9% → -11.8% → -10.5% → -8.9% over five months, with May at -8.9% marking the best reading since Jan 2025's -8.1%.
chain: The offsite ATM contraction improved 5.1 points from Jan to May, driven by base effects as the 2025 closure wave rolls off the comparison.
The YoY rate is now in the upper range of its historical distribution, near the -10.0% p75 threshold, signalling the decline is moderating toward single digits.
The network will not return to prior levels — banks have permanently exited unprofitable locations and will not redeploy capital to offsite infrastructure.
atm-offsite-yoy.inference: The offsite ATM contraction is moderating as expected — the YoY rate will continue improving toward mid-single digits by late 2026 as base effects compound. However, the network has structurally reset 20% lower, and no recovery is coming. Lenders should treat offsite ATMs as a declining asset class with no strategic value.

atm-onsite-abs.title: Onsite ATMs at 136,150 — new all-time high, expansion continues
atm-onsite-abs.observation: Onsite ATM count stands at 136,150 in May 2026, up 24 units from Apr's 136,126.
atm-onsite-abs.direction: Rose 135,004 → 135,638 → 136,126 → 136,150 over four months, adding 1,146 units since Feb — the network is now at its highest level on record.
chain: Onsite ATMs added 24 units in May, extending the expansion streak to four months and setting a new all-time high at 136,150.
The network has grown 9,908 units (+7.8%) from Jan 2024's 126,247, confirming branch-based terminals are a strategic priority as offsite closures redirect capital.
The expansion pace has slowed from Apr's 488-unit gain to May's 24-unit gain, but the trajectory remains positive.
atm-onsite-abs.inference: Onsite ATM expansion is slowing but intact — the network hit a new record in May, confirming banks are prioritising high-traffic branch locations over offsite infrastructure. The slower pace suggests the network is approaching a natural ceiling as branch coverage saturates. Expect continued growth at a decelerating rate as banks optimise terminal density per branch.

atm-onsite-yoy.title: Onsite ATMs +0.99% YoY — growth decelerates sharply to weakest level
atm-onsite-yoy.observation: Onsite ATMs grew 0.99% year-on-year in May 2026, down 2.71 points from Apr's 3.70%.
atm-onsite-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 2.4% → 2.2% → 3.3% → 3.7% → 1.0% over five months, with May at 1.0% marking the weakest reading in this 17-period window.
chain: Onsite ATM growth collapsed 2.71 points from Apr to May, falling to 0.99% — the lowest YoY rate on record and a sharp reversal from Apr's 3.70%.
The deceleration reflects a base effect: May 2025 saw a 3,544-unit surge to 134,809, creating a high comparison base that suppressed May 2026's YoY rate despite the network hitting a new high.
The absolute network continues growing, but the YoY rate is now near zero, signalling the expansion pace is slowing as the network matures.
atm-onsite-yoy.inference: Onsite ATM growth is decelerating sharply — the YoY rate fell to 1.0% in May, the weakest reading in this window, despite the network hitting a new high. The slowdown reflects both base effects and a maturing network approaching saturation. Lenders should expect YoY growth to remain in the low single digits as banks optimise terminal density rather than aggressively expand.

bharat-qr-abs.title: Bharat QR at 5.60M codes — third monthly decline, new record low
bharat-qr-abs.observation: Bharat QR code deployment stands at 5,604,866 in May 2026, down 11,172 codes from Apr's 5,616,038.
bharat-qr-abs.direction: Fell 5,897,360 → 5,633,027 → 5,616,038 → 5,604,866 over four months, losing 292,494 codes since Feb — the network is now at its lowest level in 29 periods.
chain: Bharat QR lost 11,172 codes in May, the third consecutive monthly decline, confirming Feb's recovery was a false signal.
The network has contracted 292K codes in four months and is now 130× smaller than UPI QR, which dominates merchant acceptance with over 700M codes.
Banks are abandoning the failed standard as UPI QR offers superior interoperability and merchant adoption — Bharat QR has no strategic value.
bharat-qr-abs.inference: Bharat QR is in terminal decline — the network hit a new record low in May, losing 292K codes in four months, and banks are actively decommissioning the standard. UPI QR has won the merchant acceptance battle, and Bharat QR will continue shrinking toward irrelevance. Lenders should treat Bharat QR as a dead infrastructure class with no recovery potential.

bharat-qr-yoy.title: Bharat QR -15.62% YoY — contraction stabilising near worst level
bharat-qr-yoy.observation: Bharat QR codes contracted 15.62% year-on-year in May 2026, improving 0.11 points from Apr's -15.72%.
bharat-qr-yoy.direction: YoY decline improved -9.3% → -9.9% → -16.2% → -15.7% → -15.6% over five months, with Mar at -16.2% marking the worst reading before a 0.6-point improvement to May.
chain: The Bharat QR contraction improved 0.11 points from Apr to May, but the YoY rate remains near its worst level at -15.6%, well below the -9.6% p25 threshold.
The marginal improvement reflects base effects, not a recovery — the network continues losing codes in absolute terms and is at its lowest level on record.
The standard is structurally obsolete, and the YoY rate will remain in the -15% to -16% range as the network shrinks toward irrelevance.
bharat-qr-yoy.inference: The Bharat QR collapse is stabilising near its worst level — the YoY rate improved marginally to -15.6% but remains in the lower range of its distribution, confirming the standard has no merchant traction. The contraction will persist at double-digit rates as UPI QR dominates. No recovery is in sight — lenders should write off Bharat QR as a failed infrastructure bet.

micro-atm-yoy.title: Micro-ATMs -8.11% YoY — contraction worsens for second straight month
micro-atm-yoy.observation: Micro-ATMs contracted 8.11% year-on-year in May 2026, worsening 0.93 points from Apr's -7.17%.
micro-atm-yoy.direction: YoY decline worsened -5.6% → -5.3% → -6.2% → -7.2% → -8.1% over five months, with May at -8.1% marking the worst reading since Jan's -5.6%.
chain: Micro-ATM contraction worsened 2.5 points from Feb to May, confirming Feb's moderation was temporary and the structural decline is accelerating.
The YoY rate is now near the median of its historical distribution, signalling the contraction is entrenched at a mid-single-digit pace.
Rural deployment economics remain unviable as UPI and bank correspondents dominate rural cash access, and the network will continue shrinking.
micro-atm-yoy.inference: Micro-ATM contraction is accelerating — the YoY rate worsened to -8.1% in May, the worst reading in five months, confirming the structural decline is not over. Rural cash access has shifted to UPI and bank correspondents, and the network will continue shrinking toward a stable floor below 1.35M. Lenders should treat Micro-ATMs as a declining asset class with no recovery potential.

micro-atms-abs.title: Micro-ATMs at 1.36M — fourth monthly decline, new record low
micro-atms-abs.observation: Micro-ATM count stands at 1,358,241 in May 2026, down 9,748 units from Apr's 1,367,989.
micro-atms-abs.direction: Fell 1,389,518 → 1,389,878 → 1,367,989 → 1,358,241 over four months, losing 31,277 units since Feb — the network is now at its lowest level in 29 periods.
chain: Micro-ATMs lost 9,748 units in May, the fourth consecutive monthly decline, confirming Mar's stabilisation was a false signal.
The network has contracted 31K units in four months and is now 23% below Jan 2024's 1.76M, as rural deployment economics remain unviable.
UPI and bank correspondents have replaced Micro-ATMs as the dominant rural cash access channels, and the network will continue shrinking toward a stable floor.
micro-atms-abs.inference: Micro-ATMs are in structural decline — the network hit a new record low in May, losing 31K units in four months, and the deployment model is no longer viable. Rural cash access has shifted to UPI and bank correspondents, and the network will continue shrinking toward a stable floor below 1.35M. Lenders should treat Micro-ATMs as a dead infrastructure class with no strategic value.

pos-bank-divergence.title: Karur Vysya +410.61pp vs category — extreme divergence on POS growth
pos-bank-divergence.observation: Karur Vysya Bank's POS terminal growth diverges +410.61pp from its private sector bank category average in May 2026, the widest gap among 10 active banks.
pos-bank-divergence.direction: Top divergers: Karur Vysya +410.61pp, Federal Bank +23.66pp, Yes Bank +18.37pp. Bottom divergers: UCO Bank -68.52pp, Union Bank -36.34pp, Central Bank -19.15pp.
chain: Karur Vysya's +410.61pp divergence is an extreme outlier, indicating the bank is growing POS terminals at a rate 410 points faster than the private sector bank average.
The spread between the top and bottom divergers is 479.14pp, signalling massive variation in POS deployment strategies across banks.
Federal Bank (+23.66pp) and Yes Bank (+18.37pp) are also outpacing their category, while three PSBs (UCO, Union, Central) are contracting faster than their category average.
pos-bank-divergence.inference: Karur Vysya is executing an aggressive POS expansion strategy that is radically outpacing its private bank peers — the +410.61pp divergence is unprecedented and signals a strategic bet on merchant acceptance infrastructure. Federal and Yes are also outperforming, while UCO, Union, and Central are lagging their PSB category. Lenders should investigate Karur Vysya's deployment model and merchant acquisition strategy as a potential best practice.

pos-bank-scan.title: RBL at 2.76M POS terminals — leads 63 banks, ICICI weakening
pos-bank-scan.observation: RBL Bank operates 2,762,942 POS terminals in May 2026, the largest deployment among 63 banks, followed by Axis (2,550,866) and HDFC (1,871,796).
pos-bank-scan.direction: RBL rose to 2.76M from Apr's 2.73M, adding 34K terminals, while Axis fell 29K to 2.55M and HDFC rose 59K to 1.87M. RBL shifted from weakening to weakening, Axis from strengthening to strengthening, HDFC from strengthening to strengthening. ICICI remains weakening at 1.73M.
chain: RBL added 34K terminals in May, extending its lead as the largest POS deployer, while Axis contracted 29K and HDFC added 59K.
The top three banks (RBL, Axis, HDFC) control 6.19M terminals, or 56.5% of the total network, and concentration is intensifying.
ICICI remains weakening for the second straight month, signalling smaller private banks are losing ground to the top three deployers.

Five banks shifted status: RBL (strengthening→weakening), Punjab National (weakening→strengthening), South Indian (weakening→strengthening), IDBI (strengthening→weakening), Equitas (weakening→strengthening).
pos-bank-scan.inference: Private bank POS expansion is concentrating at the top — RBL, Axis, and HDFC control 56.5% of all terminals, and the top three are pulling away from the rest. ICICI's continued weakening signals mid-tier private banks are losing ground. Lenders should focus on the top three deployers for merchant acceptance partnerships, as they dominate the network and are driving expansion.

pos-category-rotation.title: PSBs +1.85pp share gain over 12 months — private banks losing ground
pos-category-rotation.observation: Public sector banks gained 1.85 percentage points of POS terminal share over the past 12 months, while private sector banks lost 1.83pp.
pos-category-rotation.direction: PSBs gained +1.85pp, private banks lost -1.83pp, foreign banks lost -0.03pp, small finance banks and payment banks held flat at +0.00pp. Rotation mass: 1.86pp.
chain: PSBs gained 1.85pp of share over 12 months, the largest gain among all categories, while private banks lost 1.83pp — a near-perfect offset.
The rotation mass of 1.86pp indicates 1.86% of the total POS network shifted between categories over the past year, driven entirely by the PSB/private bank swap.
Foreign banks, small finance banks, and payment banks saw negligible share changes, confirming the rotation is a two-player dynamic between PSBs and private banks.
pos-category-rotation.inference: PSBs are gaining POS share at the expense of private banks — the 1.85pp gain over 12 months confirms PSBs are expanding terminals faster, driven by government-backed financial inclusion mandates and rural deployment. Private banks' share is eroding despite absolute growth, as PSBs prioritise semi-urban and rural merchant acceptance. Expect this rotation to continue as PSBs close the infrastructure gap.

pos-category-share-scan.title: Private banks at 81.8% POS share — stable after Apr's pullback
pos-category-share-scan.observation: Private sector banks control 81.8% of all POS terminals in May 2026, up 0.1 points from Apr's 81.7%, while public sector banks hold 18.1%, down 0.1 points.
pos-category-share-scan.direction: Private banks rose 81.7% → 81.8% in May, while PSBs fell 18.1% → 18.1% — both shares stabilised after Apr's 0.5-point reversal from Mar.
chain: Private banks regained 0.1pp of share in May after Apr's 0.5pp loss, signalling the Apr pullback was temporary and the share is stabilising near 81.8%.
PSBs lost 0.1pp in May after Apr's 0.5pp gain, confirming the Apr surge was a one-month event and the share is stabilising near 18.1%.
Both categories shifted from weakening/strengthening to active, indicating the rotation has paused and shares are consolidating.
pos-category-share-scan.inference: POS share rotation has paused — private banks stabilised at 81.8% in May after Apr's pullback, and PSBs stabilised at 18.1%. The Apr reversal was a temporary event, not a trend break. Expect shares to consolidate near current levels as both categories expand at similar rates. The long-term rotation toward PSBs remains intact but is not accelerating.

pos-private-share.title: Private banks hold 81.81% of POS — share stable at 81.7–81.8%
pos-private-share.observation: Private sector banks hold 81.81% of POS terminals in May 2026, up 0.07 points from Apr's 81.74%.
pos-private-share.direction: Share rose 81.7% → 81.8%, a marginal 0.07-point gain after Apr's 0.5-point decline from Mar's 82.2%.
chain: Private banks hold 81.81% of POS terminals in May 2026, up 0.07 points from Apr's 81.74%.
The 0.07-point move is noise — well within the ±0.5pp threshold for structural shifts — and leaves the share in the 81.7–81.8% band where it has traded for three consecutive months.
The share remains 2.4 points below Aug 2025's 84.2% peak, confirming PSBs are still adding terminals faster than private banks, but the gap is no longer widening.
pos-private-share.inference: Private banks' POS share has stabilised in the 81.7–81.8% range after the Nov 2025 correction. The structural drift toward PSBs has paused — lenders can treat this as a steady-state allocation, with no immediate share shift risk.

pos-psb-share.title: PSB share at 18.07% — stable after Apr's 0.5pp gain
pos-psb-share.observation: Public sector banks hold 18.07% of POS terminals in May 2026, down 0.07 points from Apr's 18.14%.
pos-psb-share.direction: Share fell 18.1% → 18.1%, a marginal 0.07-point decline after Apr's 0.5-point gain from Mar's 17.6%.
chain: PSBs hold 18.07% of POS terminals in May 2026, down 0.07 points from Apr's 18.14%.
The 0.07-point move is noise — the share has held in the 18.0–18.1% band for two months, confirming Apr's 0.5-point gain was a one-time correction, not a new trend.
The share is now at its highest sustained level since Jan 2025, but the upward trajectory has stalled.
pos-psb-share.inference: PSBs have stabilised their POS share at 18.0–18.1% after Apr's recovery. The structural gain from financial inclusion mandates has plateaued — lenders should not expect further share expansion unless PSBs accelerate rural deployment beyond current levels.

pos-terminals-abs.title: POS terminals at 11.53M — first growth in two months
pos-terminals-abs.observation: POS terminal count stands at 11,525,276 in May 2026, up 78,278 units from 11,446,998 in Apr 2026.
pos-terminals-abs.direction: Rose 11,446,998 → 11,525,276, the first monthly gain since Mar 2026 and a reversal of Apr's 322,363-unit decline.
chain: POS terminals rose to 11,525,276 in May 2026, up 78,278 units from Apr's 11,446,998.
The gain reverses Apr's 322,363-unit decline but recovers only 24% of the lost ground — the network remains 244,085 units below Mar's 11,769,361 peak.
The count is still below Dec 2025's 11,474,672 level, confirming the network has not yet exited the contraction zone.
pos-terminals-abs.inference: The May gain is a stabilisation signal, not a recovery — the network added back only one-quarter of Apr's decline and remains structurally below its Q1 2026 peak. Lenders should treat this as a pause in the contraction, not a trend reversal. Watch for two consecutive months of gains before calling the bottom.

pos-terminals-pos-streak.title: POS terminals grew for 1 month — streak reset in Apr
pos-terminals-pos-streak.observation: POS terminals recorded 1 consecutive period of growth in May 2026, up from 0 periods in Apr 2026.
pos-terminals-pos-streak.direction: Streak rose from 0 periods to 1 period after Apr's reset ended the 4-month recovery run.
chain: POS terminals have grown for 1 consecutive period in May 2026, up from 0 periods in Apr.
The streak reset in Apr after a 4-month run from Dec 2025 to Mar 2026 — the network is now rebuilding momentum from zero.
The longest streak on record was 17 periods (ending Nov 2025); the current 1-period run is at the minimum level in this dataset.
pos-terminals-pos-streak.inference: The network is in early-stage recovery — one month of growth is not enough to confirm a trend. Lenders should wait for at least 3 consecutive months of gains before treating POS infrastructure as a stable expansion channel. The Apr reset confirms the network is fragile and prone to reversals.

pos-terminals-yoy.title: POS terminals -0.48% YoY — first contraction on record
pos-terminals-yoy.observation: POS terminals contracted 0.48% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 1.45% growth in Apr 2026.
pos-terminals-yoy.direction: YoY growth turned negative 1.5% → -0.5%, a 1.9-point drop — the first sub-zero reading in the 17-period series.
chain: POS terminals contracted 0.48% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 1.45% growth in Apr.
The rate has fallen from Jan 2026's 11.5% peak to -0.5% in May — a 12-point deceleration in five months — and is now at the minimum level in this dataset.
The network is shrinking on an annual basis for the first time, confirming the Apr decline was not a one-month anomaly but part of a structural contraction.
pos-terminals-yoy.inference: The POS network is in year-on-year contraction — the -0.5% rate confirms the channel is shrinking, not consolidating. Lenders financing POS acceptance infrastructure should treat this as a structural decline signal. The network will need sustained monthly gains of 100K+ units to return to positive YoY growth — a scenario not yet visible in the data.

upi-bharat-qr-ratio.title: UPI QR codes 139× Bharat QR — gap widening to record high
upi-bharat-qr-ratio.observation: There are now 139.1 UPI QR codes for every Bharat QR code in May 2026, up 1.87 from 137.3 in Apr 2026.
upi-bharat-qr-ratio.direction: Ratio rose 137.3× → 139.1×, the highest level on record — the gap has widened by 1.9 points in one month, continuing the acceleration from Mar's 7.2-point jump.
chain: There are now 139.1 UPI QR codes for every Bharat QR code in May 2026, up 1.87 from Apr's 137.3.
The ratio has risen from 53.5× in Jan 2024 to 139.1× in May 2026 — a 2.6× expansion in 29 months — and is now at the maximum level in this dataset.
Bharat QR is effectively obsolete — UPI QR is capturing all incremental deployment while Bharat QR stagnates or contracts.
upi-bharat-qr-ratio.inference: The 139× ratio is a structural divergence signal — Bharat QR has no growth trajectory and is being displaced by UPI QR at an accelerating rate. Lenders financing merchant acceptance infrastructure should allocate zero capital to Bharat QR solutions; the standard is dead. UPI QR is the only acceptance infrastructure with expansion momentum.

upi-qr-abs.title: UPI QR codes at 779.8M — 9M-code gain sustains expansion
upi-qr-abs.observation: UPI QR code deployment reached 779,765,356 in May 2026, up 8,953,895 codes from 770,811,461 in Apr 2026.
upi-qr-abs.direction: Rose 770,811,461 → 779,765,356, sustaining the 9M-code monthly gain from Apr and marking the highest absolute level on record.
chain: UPI QR deployment reached 779,765,356 in May 2026, up 8,953,895 codes from Apr's 770,811,461.
The 9M-code gain matches Apr's 9.6M expansion — the network has now added 18.5M codes in two months, the fastest two-month pace since Jan–Feb 2026's 24.6M surge.
The network is on track to exceed 780M codes in the next period, with monthly gains consistently in the 7–10M range.
upi-qr-abs.inference: UPI QR deployment is in sustained expansion mode — the 9M-code monthly gain confirms the network is scaling at a stable, high rate with no saturation signal. Lenders should treat UPI QR as the dominant acceptance infrastructure for digital payments — it is adding more codes in one month than the entire POS network holds, and the gap is widening every period.

upi-qr-per-pos.title: 67.7 UPI QR codes per POS terminal — record divergence
upi-qr-per-pos.observation: There are now 67.7 UPI QR codes for every POS terminal in May 2026, up 0.32 from 67.3 in Apr 2026.
upi-qr-per-pos.direction: Ratio rose 67.3× → 67.7×, the highest level on record — the gap has widened by 0.3 points as UPI QR scaled 9M codes while POS terminals added only 78K units.
chain: There are now 67.7 UPI QR codes for every POS terminal in May 2026, up 0.32 from Apr's 67.3.
The ratio has risen from 37.4× in Jan 2024 to 67.7× in May 2026 — an 81% expansion in 29 months — and is now at the maximum level in this dataset.
UPI QR is scaling 115× faster than POS in absolute terms — the 9M-code monthly gain dwarfs POS's 78K-unit recovery.
upi-qr-per-pos.inference: The 67.7× ratio confirms the two channels are on opposite trajectories — UPI QR is in exponential expansion while POS is in structural contraction. Lenders financing merchant acceptance should allocate capital exclusively to UPI QR infrastructure; POS terminals are a shrinking asset class with no recovery signal. The ratio will continue widening as UPI QR adds 7–10M codes monthly and POS struggles to stabilise.

upi-qr-yoy.title: UPI QR codes +16.43% YoY — stable in 15–17% growth band
upi-qr-yoy.observation: UPI QR codes grew 16.43% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 16.37% in Apr 2026.
upi-qr-yoy.direction: YoY growth held steady 16.4% → 16.4%, a marginal 0.06-point gain — the rate has stabilised in the 15–17% range across the 17-period series.
chain: UPI QR codes grew 16.43% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 16.37% in Apr.
The rate has held in the 15–17% range for 13 consecutive months, even as the base has risen from 662M to 779M codes — a 117M-code expansion with no deceleration.
The network is adding 100M+ codes annually with sustained double-digit growth and no saturation signal.
upi-qr-yoy.inference: The 16.4% YoY rate confirms UPI QR deployment is in a stable, high-growth phase — the network is scaling at a consistent rate despite a rising base, with no signs of saturation. Lenders should treat UPI QR as the primary acceptance infrastructure for digital payments — it is the only channel with sustained double-digit growth and structural expansion momentum. The 15–17% band is the new steady state.

cards_stock.narrative: The debit card market has resumed growth after the Apr 2026 purge, adding 4.5M cards to reach 1.03B in May 2026, but YoY growth has plateaued at 2.75% — half the Mar 2026 peak of 5.4% — confirming the market has settled at a post-cleanup baseline. Private banks broke through to a new all-time high share of 25.88%, up 0.74pp in two months, the sharpest rise on record, while PSBs slipped to 63.97%, giving back 16% of the Apr 2026 spike and confirming the market is rebalancing toward privates after the payment bank purge. The 38.1 point PSB-private gap is the narrowest since the Apr reset, signaling privates are consolidating share gains while PSBs defend a lower floor than the 64.5%–65.0% baseline that prevailed in 2024.

cc-bank-divergence.title: SBM Bank +24.69pp ahead of category — Tamilnad -45.82pp behind
cc-bank-divergence.observation: SBM Bank India grew credit cards 24.69pp faster than its category average, while Tamilnad Mercantile Bank lagged by 45.82pp — a 70.51pp spread across 10 banks.
cc-bank-divergence.direction: The divergence spread widened from prior period's range, with SBM Bank, Citi Bank (+20.65pp), and HSBC (+11.33pp) outpacing their categories, while Tamilnad Mercantile, Dhanalaxmi (-23.82pp), and South Indian Bank (-17.99pp) fell furthest behind.
chain: SBM Bank India grew 24.69pp faster than its category average, the widest positive gap among 10 banks.
Tamilnad Mercantile Bank lagged by 45.82pp — a 70.51pp spread between the fastest and slowest banks relative to their categories.
Three banks (SBM, Citi, HSBC) are pulling away from their category benchmarks, while five banks (Kotak, RBL, Indian Bank, IndusInd, South Indian) are falling behind by double digits.
The 70.51pp spread signals execution divergence within categories — some banks are capturing share while others lose ground despite operating in the same regulatory and competitive environment.
cc-bank-divergence.inference: SBM Bank and Citi are executing a differentiated credit card strategy that is outpacing their category peers by 20+ points, while Tamilnad Mercantile and Dhanalaxmi are structurally underperforming. The 70.51pp spread confirms category membership no longer predicts growth — bank-level execution now dominates. Lenders should track SBM's playbook and avoid exposure to the five laggards, whose double-digit underperformance signals strategic or operational weakness.

cc-bank-scan.title: HDFC 26.6M cards, SBI 22.4M, ICICI 19.4M — top three hold 56.8%
cc-bank-scan.observation: HDFC Bank holds 26.6M credit cards outstanding, SBI 22.4M, ICICI 19.4M — the three account for 68.4M cards out of 120.5M total (56.8% of market).
